mnemonics.title: EPDF - Every Process Does Flow
mnemonics.content: E-P-D-F: External entity provides input → Process transforms data → Data flows between them → Data store holds information. Remember: Every Process Drives Flow!

mnemonics.title: FAD - Flows Are Directional
mnemonics.content: FAD: Flow Always Directional. Data flows in one direction only with arrow notation. Two-way communication requires two separate data flow lines with arrows pointing each direction.

mnemonics.title: No Circles In Process - NPIP
mnemonics.content: NPIP: No Picture In Process. Processes must have at least one input and one output flow. A process cannot have only inputs (black hole) or only outputs (miracle/white hole).

mnemonics.title: Level Order - Cats Prefer Mice
mnemonics.content: C-P-M: Context diagram (Level 0) → Process 1 (Level 1) → More detailed processes (Level 2). Think: Cats Prefer Mice as you go deeper!

mnemonics.title: Data Store Rules - DSR
mnemonics.content: DSR: Data can flow FROM a data store only when retrieved, TO a data store only when stored. Data cannot flow directly from one data store to another - must go through a process.

acronyms.usage: Use when representing the flow of data through a system graphically. It's the primary modeling tool for flow-oriented approach.
Use Level 0 (zero) DFD to show the system as a single process with all external entities. Shows only major data flows between system and environment.

acronyms.expansion: External Entity, Process, Data Flow, Data Store
acronyms.usage: The four building blocks of any DFD. Use to remember all components that can appear in a data flow diagram.

acronyms.expansion: A popular DFD notation style using specific symbols
acronyms.usage: Use this notation when drawing DFDs: Square for External Entity, Rounded rectangle for Process, Open-ended rectangle for Data Store, Arrow for Data Flow.
Use when preferring circle/rounded bubble for Process, stick arrow for Data Flow notation.

visualTips.visualization: Draw a SQUARE - Think of it as the SOURCE (origin) or SINK (destination). It's outside your system boundary like a customer, supplier, or another system. Visualize people standing outside a factory gate.
Draw a ROUNDED RECTANGLE or CIRCLE - Represents WORK being done, data being TRANSFORMED. Visualize a machine in the factory that takes raw material (input) and produces finished goods (output).
Draw an OPEN RECTANGLE (two parallel lines) - Represents STORAGE like a database or file. Visualize a filing cabinet or warehouse where data is kept for later use.
Draw an ARROW with a LABEL - Shows the PATH of data movement. The arrow direction shows which way data travels. Label clearly what data is flowing. Think of conveyor belts in a factory.
Draw a large circle representing the entire system in the center, with external entities surrounding it. Only show major data flows in and out. Like a bird's eye view of a city.

Imagine water flowing through pipes - what enters a lower level must exit. The number of input/output flows at Level N must equal the flows at Level N+1. Keep a running tally: INPUTS = OUTPUTS + STORES.

analogies.analogy: Think of a city MAP showing how goods (data) move: Suppliers (External Entities) send goods → Warehouses (Data Stores) store them → Factories (Processes) transform them → Customers (External Entities) receive finished products. The roads with arrows show how goods flow between these points.

Like a KITCHEN BLENDER: You put fruits in (input), the blender processes/changes them, and you get juice (output). A process must have ingredients to work with and produce something - it can't create output from nothing!

Think of a LIBRARY or WAREHOUSE - books/data come in, get catalogued and stored, then checked out when needed. Data sits there until a process requests it. The library itself doesn't change the book, just holds it.

Think of a company's ORGANIZATIONAL CHART: The CEO (Level 0) oversees the whole company. Department heads (Level 1) report to CEO. Managers under departments (Level 2) report to department heads. What flows to/from departments must match what flows to/from their managers combined.

Think of the TICKET COUNTER at a movie theater or railway station - people (data) come from outside, interact with the system, and leave. The ticket counter is the process, but the people are external entities who initiate or receive the final output.
Data flow is like WATER flowing through pipes - it carries information. Control flow is like the VALVE that decides WHEN water flows. DFD only models data (what), not control logic (when/how).
